Article.ArticleTitle: Disrupted intrathalamic and thalamocortical structural covariance networks in posttraumatic stress disorder.

Abstract.AbstractText: The thalamus is a heterogeneous structure crucial for corticocortical communication, affective-perceptual integration, motor preparation, and memory-related functions. Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is characterized by various symptoms that likely relate to thalamic functions. Group-level and individual differential structural covariance (SC) analyses were conducted on intrathalamic, thalamocortical, and thalamosubcortical volumetric networks by segmenting structural MRI data from 2,784 subjects (PTSD n = 1,306; controls n = 1,478) into 25 thalamic nuclei per hemisphere. We found that PTSD was associated with stronger intrathalamic and thalamocortical network strength and stronger SC between the limbic thalamus and the somatomotor and auditory thalamus. PTSD severity was related to specific regional alterations in the intrathalamic network involving the lateral pulvinar. Comorbid depression severity positively correlated with global intrathalamic alterations, while avoidance symptoms positively correlated with global thalamosubcortical alterations. Hyperarousal symptoms related to altered SC in the thalamocortical network between the reuniens, central medial, paratenial, centromedian, and limitans-suprageniculate nuclei and lateral cortical regions spanning the occipital, temporal, and orbitofrontal cortices. Differential associations between avoidance, hyperarousal, and comorbid depression symptoms and thalamic SC in PTSD suggest that specific thalamic covariance patterns may be involved in unique facets of PTSD symptomatology.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: The thalamus has been increasingly implicated in psychiatric disorders, including posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). However, most studies on PTSD have analyzed the thalamus as a whole, overlooking the distinct functional roles of its nuclei in cognitive, affective, and sensorimotor processes. This study examined structural covariance (SC) patterns of individual thalamic nuclei and found that PTSD diagnosis was related to differences in thalamic SC. While traditional studies of SC occur at the group level, we additionally conducted a subject-level SC analysis, which showed that distinct aspects of thalamic SC were linked to different PTSD symptoms. Comorbid depression was related to intrathalamic alterations, avoidance symptoms to thalamosubcortical alterations, and hyperarousal symptoms to thalamocortical alterations. This work should inform future research examining thalamic nuclei in relation to PTSD and other psychopathologies.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Competing Interests: See Conflict of Interests statement. LAML reports unpaid membership on the Scientific Committee for the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation (ISSTD), grant support from the National Institute of Mental Health, K01 MH118467, and spousal IP payments from Vanderbilt University for technology licensed to Acadia Pharmaceuticals unrelated to the present work. ISSTD and NIMH were not involved in the analysis or preparation of the manuscript. R.J.D. is the founder and president of the board of directors and serves on the board of directors for the nonprofit organization Healthy Minds Innovations, Inc. C.G.A. has served as a consultant, speaker, and/or an advisory board for Douglas Pharmaceutical, Aptinyx, FSV7, Lundbeck, Psilocybin Labs, Genentech, and Janssen. C.G.A. filed a patent for using mechanistic Target of Rapamycin (mTOR) inhibitors to augment the effects of antidepressants (filed on August 20, 2018). W.E.H.: Air Liquide, CHUGAI, EISAI, Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Janssen, Lundbeck, Otsuka, UCB, none related to this work.
